cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is about what the author believes based on the passage provided. I need to read the passage carefully and figure out the correct answer. First, let's summarize the main points of the passage. The author starts by talking about different hobbies and interests people have, like collecting coins, needlework, sports. Then they mention that reading tastes differ too—some people read newspapers, novels, or books on specific topics. The author points out that people have different interests and that's okay. Then they ask if there's something that interests everyone, and say yes, there are questions that should interest everyone, which the course is about. The author then discusses basic needs like food, warmth, and company, but when those are satisfied, there's something else everyone needs: to figure out who we are and why we are here. The passage emphasizes that this is a fundamental need beyond just physical or social needs, and that philosophers believe this. The author also says that this question is more important than things like winning Olympic medals. Looking at the options: A) No existing subject can interest everyone in the world. But the author says there are questions that should interest everyone, so A is probably incorrect. B) Different people may have different interests and concerns. The passage does mention that people have different interests (sports, reading materials, etc.), but the author is also making a point that there is a common concern—figuring out existence. So B is true but maybe not the main point here. C) Everyone has to figure out who we are and why we are here. This is directly stated in the passage: "that is to figure out who we are and why we are here." The author emphasizes that this is a universal need beyond basic needs, so C seems correct. D) People in modern society pay more attention to philosophical questions. The passage doesn't mention anything about modern society versus other times. It talks about a debate as long as humans have existed, but not specifically about modern times paying more
